title: Frontend Architecture Principles
summary: Keep business rules independent from rendering, frameworks, and delivery mechanisms.
purpose: Architecture principles give the team a shared way to reason about code before choosing folders or libraries.
rule: Feature code should separate UI, orchestration, domain decisions, data mapping, and infrastructure access. Dependencies point inward toward stable domain concepts, not outward toward screens, API clients, or native modules.
whenToUse: Creating a feature that includes UI, API data, state, and business rules.
Refactoring components that contain validation, mapping, or permissions logic.
Deciding whether code belongs in a feature, domain, infrastructure, presentation, or shared layer.
whenNotToUse: For tiny static UI with no state, side effects, or business behavior.
For experiments that have an explicit deletion date.

recommended.title: Orchestrate behavior outside the view

avoid.title: Mix API calls, mapping, and UI

why: When rendering code owns business rules, every UI change becomes risky and every backend change leaks into screens. The principle is simple: stable decisions go inward, volatile details stay outward. UI, APIs, storage, navigation, analytics, and native APIs are delivery mechanisms; they should depend on product rules, not define them.
checklist: UI renders view models, not raw API DTOs.
Business rules are testable without rendering a component.
Shared code does not import feature-specific code.
Framework-specific details stay near the app boundary.
Infrastructure code implements contracts instead of being imported directly by UI.
